10: The Shunammite’s hospitality to Elisha — constructing a dedicated ʿaliyyat qîr (a walled upper room with a bed, table, chair, and lamp) — reflects both the ANE hospitality tradition for wandering holy men and the particular Israelite institution of the ʾîš ʾelōhîm (man of God) as a recognized social-religious figure supported by wealthy patrons. The ʿaliyyāh (upper room/chamber) was a distinct room typically built on the roof or as a second story, providing the most private and prestigious space in the house; the furnishing details (bed, table, chair, lamp) indicate a permanent guest room for regular use, not temporary hospitality. Comparable ANE parallels: the Ugaritic text KTU 1.17 depicts the hero Danel receiving a divine oracle after offering hospitality to the divine messengers, establishing the reciprocal pattern of hospitality-to-divine-agent yielding divine provision. The Elijah/Zarephath widow parallel (1 Kings 17:19-23: Elijah brought to the widow’s upper room, raises her son there) establishes the ʿaliyyāh as the space for the greatest miracle: the son raised in the upper room of the man of God. Both ‘upper room’ episodes anticipate the NT’s upper room (Mark 14:15: anagaion) where the Last Supper is held, and Acts 1:13 where the disciples await the Spirit — the upper room as the space of covenant renewal and resurrection presence.
1: The woman identifies her husband as a member of the b’nê hannebî’îm, ‘sons of the prophets’ — a prophetic guild community associated with Elisha. These were communal groups (cf. 2 Kings 6:1–7) who lived under the supervision of a prophetic leader; their members were not necessarily blood relatives of Elijah or Elisha but disciples or associates. The legal backdrop is Exodus 21:7 (and Leviticus 25:39–41): Israelite debt-slavery law permitted a creditor to seize a debtor’s children as bond-servants when the debt could not be paid. The widow’s appeal to Elisha is a patronage appeal — she invokes her husband’s loyalty to the prophetic community as a claim on Elisha’s intercession.
2: Olive oil was a primary commodity in the ancient Near East — used for cooking, lighting (lamp fuel), cosmetics, medicinal purposes, and temple ritual. The widow’s single jar (ʾāsûḵ, a container for anointing oil) is a marginal supply, barely enough for personal use. Elisha’s instructions to gather empty vessels from neighbors engage the village economy: such borrowing was a recognized social practice in which community solidarity partially buffered individual economic collapse.
3: Elisha’s instruction to borrow ‘empty containers’ and ‘not too few’ (ʾal-tamʿîṭî) frames the miracle as contingent on the widow’s faith and initiative — the number of vessels gathered would determine the volume of oil produced. This structure — where the scope of divine provision tracks the human act of prepared receptivity — appears also in the widow of Zarephath account (1 Kgs 17) and in Gospel multiplication narratives. ANE parallel: the goddess Asherah was associated with oil abundance, but the text deliberately attributes the provision to YHWH through the prophetic word alone.
4: The instruction to ‘shut the door behind you and your sons’ withdraws the miracle from public view — a pattern in Elisha’s ministry (cf. 2 Kings 4:33, where he also shuts the door in the Shunammite resurrection account). The closed-door setting emphasizes that the miracle is not a public spectacle or a demonstration of prophetic power but a private act of provision. The woman’s sons function as assistants; their presence links the miracle to the family unit that would otherwise be enslaved.
5: The narrative slows to emphasize repetition — ‘they kept bringing vessels while she kept pouring’ — using the Hebrew imperfect to convey continued action. The scene is entirely domestic: mother and sons working in an enclosed space with borrowed household equipment. The extraordinary emerges within the ordinary rhythms of village labor. Neighbors’ vessels represent the entire social network the family could access; when those are exhausted, the provision ceases.
6: The oil stops precisely when the last borrowed vessel is filled — not before, not after. This detail underlines the interpretive framework established by v3: the scale of provision matched the scope of preparation. The widow’s statement ‘there are no more vessels’ functions as the natural limiting condition; the miracle operates within the constraints of the human response. Comparable ANE royal inscriptions sometimes attribute agricultural abundance to divine favor, but those are idealized propaganda; this account is intimate and economically specific.
7: Elisha’s solution is practical: sell the oil, pay the debt, live on the surplus. The language of debt-payment (šallᵉmî ʾet-niššayēḵ) uses the same root (šālem) as shalom — debt cancellation as a form of restoration and wholeness. The surplus oil that remains after debt payment provides ongoing subsistence for the widow and her sons. The miracle does not make the family wealthy; it cancels their liability and re-establishes their social standing in the community.
8: Shunem (modern Sulam) is a village in the Jezreel Valley in Issachar, some 15–20 km from Carmel. A ‘great woman’ (ʾiššâ gᵉdôlāh) denotes high social standing — wealth, land-ownership, and community influence, not merely personal virtue. Her pressing Elisha to eat (using a verb of urgency, ḥāzaq) is hospitality initiative consistent with ANE aristocratic norms: receiving a known prophet was an act of honor and an invitation to divine favor. The Shunammite becomes a patron to the itinerant prophet.
9: The Shunammite’s declaration that Elisha is ‘a holy man of God’ (ʾîš ’ĕlōhîm qādôš hûʾ) uses the full honorific that marks prophetic office. The term qādôš (holy) is rare as a human descriptor in the Hebrew Bible — it signals that Elisha is set apart for divine service in a way that is perceptible to a discerning observer. The Shunammite’s theological discernment contrasts with the king’s and other officials’ tendency to miss the prophetic presence; she acts on what she perceives.
11: The guest room Elisha uses when passing through Shunem becomes the setting for the subsequent death-and-resurrection narrative (2 Kings 4:18–37). The narrative marker ‘one day he came there, went up to the room, and rested’ establishes the routine of his visits. The prophet’s reliance on the Shunammite’s hospitality inverts normal patron-client dynamics: she provides; he will ultimately provide the gift she did not ask for (a son) and cannot replace (the son’s life).
12: Elisha communicates through Gehazi his servant rather than directly with the Shunammite woman — a protocol detail that reflects ancient hierarchies. Speaking through an intermediary maintains social distance appropriate to a single male prophet and a married woman; Gehazi becomes the official channel of communication. The Shunammite’s social standing is evident: she ‘stood before him’ (wattaʿămod lᵉpānāyw) — the posture of a social equal presenting herself at a formal audience, not the posture of a supplicant.
13: Elisha’s offer to intercede with the king or the commander of the army reveals the prophet’s access to royal and military networks. The offer implies that the Shunammite woman might have legal or property disputes that required royal adjudication — a common anxiety for a wealthy woman in an honor-shame society where land tenure depended on male advocacy. Her reply ‘I dwell among my own people’ (bᵉtôḵ ʿammî ʾānōḵî yōšābet) is a declaration that her social situation is secure; she does not need political patronage.
14: Gehazi identifies the Shunammite’s barrenness and her husband’s old age — the double obstacle to an heir that placed her in a position of social vulnerability despite her current prosperity. Childlessness in the ancient Near East meant the eventual loss of property (no male heir to inherit), social marginalization in widowhood, and the extinction of the husband’s family name. The situation precisely mirrors the Abraham-Sarah narrative (Genesis 18:10–14) and the Hannah narrative (1 Sam 1); barrenness resolved by prophetic word is a recurring structural pattern in Hebrew narrative.
15: Elisha calls the Shunammite and she ‘stood in the doorway’ (wattaʿămod bappetaḥ) — the threshold position that marks liminal encounters in Hebrew narrative. Standing at a doorway signals that the encounter is formal and momentous; the same posture appears at thresholds in other prophetic narratives. The shift from Gehazi as intermediary to Elisha addressing her directly signals that what follows transcends ordinary social exchange.
16: The Shunammite’s protest ‘do not deceive your servant’ (ʾal-tᵉkazzēb bᵉšipḥātekā) is not unbelief but appropriate caution: in the ANE world, raising and then dashing a barren woman’s hope of a child was a recognized cruelty. She is protecting herself from disappointment. The word for deceive (kāzab) is the opposite of prophetic truthfulness; she is, in effect, asking Elisha to confirm that this is a genuine prophetic word and not false consolation. The language echoes the Abrahamic annunciation where Sarah also expresses incredulity (Genesis 18:12–15).
17: The fulfillment is dated to ‘about this time next year’ (kāʿēt ḥayyâ) — the same idiom used in Genesis 18:10 and 14 for the Isaac annunciation. The verbal echo signals that the narrator is deliberately placing this account in the trajectory of the Abrahamic barrenness-and-birth pattern. The Shunammite becomes another figure in the series of women — Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah — whose barrenness is overcome by divine promise and whose sons carry forward significant roles in Israel’s story.
18: The child grows to the age when he can accompany his father to the harvest field — an economic unit of participation typical of agrarian societies. Children in ANE agricultural households participated in harvest work from an early age; the harvest scene establishes a normal, functioning family life before catastrophe strikes. The juxtaposition of the fulfilled promise (a growing child) with the sudden collapse (2 Kings 4:19–20) intensifies the narrative tension.
19: ‘My head! My head!’ (rōʾšî rōʾšî) — the doubled cry signals acute distress; in the harvest field context, heatstroke is the most plausible medical cause. Harvest work in the Jezreel Valley in summer involves intense sun exposure; sudden collapse from heat injury was a recognized danger. The father’s instruction to carry the child to his mother follows expected domestic protocol: medical crises within the household were managed in the women’s sphere.
20: The child dies at noon — the time of maximum heat in the agricultural day. The child’s death ‘on her lap’ establishes the most intimate possible setting; the Shunammite is positioned as both caregiver and the one who must now act. Her response — going up to the prophet’s room and laying the child on Elisha’s bed (v21) — is a deliberate prophetic claim: the place associated with divine presence is the appropriate location for a miracle she has not yet stopped expecting.
21: Placing the dead child on the prophet’s bed is a significant act — it appropriates the sacred space of the man of God for a petition that has not yet been spoken. The Shunammite does not mourn publicly or prepare the child for burial; she acts as one who believes the story is not finished. ANE parallels: the beds and sleeping chambers of holy men were treated as spaces of concentrated divine power; placing the dead there could be understood as making a ritual claim on that power.
22: The Shunammite’s request for a servant and donkey frames a journey of about 25–30 km to Carmel — not a quick trip. Her instruction to send ‘quickly’ (ûmāhartî wᵉhālaḵtî) establishes urgency without explaining why. She conceals her purpose from her husband, whose practical objection follows in v23. The donkey as transport animal is consistent with her elevated social standing; it was the standard vehicle for persons of means in the Israelite period.
23: The husband’s question ‘Why go to him today? It is neither a new moon nor a Sabbath’ reveals that pilgrimages to prophets typically occurred on festival days — new moon (ḥōdeš) and Sabbath (šabbāt) were the recognized times for consulting a man of God. This detail illuminates the calendar of prophetic accessibility in monarchic Israel: prophets were not always available; they received visitors on holy days. The Shunammite’s visit on an ordinary day marks the emergency she refuses to name.
24: The Shunammite’s instruction to the servant ‘do not slow down unless I tell you’ reflects the urgency of the situation. The word for ‘slow down’ (ʿāṣōr baʿădî rᵉḵōb’) concerns restraining the donkey’s pace; she is pressing for maximum speed within the limits of the animal. The overland route from Shunem to Mount Carmel crosses the Jezreel Valley — perhaps four to six hours of travel. Every element of her preparation is purposeful and urgent.
25: Mount Carmel had long associations with prophetic activity (cf. 1 Kgs 18, the Elijah-Baal contest) and apparently served as one of Elisha’s regular stations. The sight of the Shunammite ‘coming in the distance’ (seen from the high ground of Carmel) prompted Elisha’s prophetic discernment that something was wrong: ‘Her soul is bitter’ (wᵉnapšāh mārat lāh, v27). Prophetic intuition of spiritual distress is a standard feature of the prophetic narratives; Elisha perceives what Gehazi cannot.
26: Elisha sends Gehazi ahead with a standard welfare query: is it well with you (hăšālôm lāḵ)? The triple inquiry — ‘with you? with your husband? with the child?’ — follows the conventional pattern of village greeting that moved through the household unit. The Shunammite’s response ‘All is well (šālôm)’ is a deliberate suspension of disclosure — she is reserving her petition for Elisha himself, not his intermediary, even as she speaks the opposite of the truth about the child’s condition.
27: The Shunammite’s seizure of Elisha’s feet (wattaḥăzēq bᵉraglāyw) is the posture of urgent petition — prostration at the feet of a superior. Gehazi’s impulse to push her away reflects protocol norms; such contact with a holy man was considered inappropriate without invitation. Elisha’s counter-instruction ‘leave her alone’ overrides normal social restraint and signals that the prophetic encounter is to proceed without barriers. His acknowledgment that YHWH has ‘hidden it from me’ is a rare statement of prophetic limitation.
28: The Shunammite’s reproach ‘Did I ask my lord for a son?’ recalls her initial caution at v16. She had warned against false hope; now the fulfilled promise has been reversed. Her complaint is formally a qal waḥomer (a fortiori) argument: if I did not request this gift, how much more grievous is its loss? The theological tension — God gave, God (through the prophet) apparently took away — is not resolved here; it is the engine driving the subsequent miracle.
29: Elisha’s instruction to Gehazi to ‘tuck your garment into your belt’ (ḥăgōr motnêḵā) is the standard preparation for urgent travel in the ancient world — gathering up the long robe to free the legs for running. The prohibition on greeting anyone en route reflects an urgency that overrides normal social courtesy (cf. Luke 10:4, where Jesus uses similar language for the seventy). The staff as an extension of prophetic power (cf. Moses’ staff; Elisha’s own earlier use in 2 Kings 2:14) makes Gehazi’s mission a preliminary attempt at mediated restoration.
30: The Shunammite’s oath ‘As the LORD lives and as you yourself live’ (ḥay-yhwh wᵉḥay-napšᵉkā) is the strongest available oath formula in Hebrew; it invokes the life of God and the prophet’s own life as guarantors that she will not be dismissed with a proxy. Her refusal to let Elisha remain on Carmel without accompanying her is a demand for direct prophetic presence — not mediated power through a staff. Elisha complies; personal accompaniment becomes necessary for the miracle.
31: Gehazi’s staff laid on the boy’s face produces no response — ‘no sound and no sign of life’ (wᵉqôl wᵉʾên qešeb). The failure of the proximate, impersonal method establishes that the resurrection requires the prophet’s direct bodily presence. This structural pattern — failed preliminary attempt followed by successful personal intervention — appears also in 1 Kings 17:21 (Elijah’s resurrection of the Zarephath widow’s son), indicating shared narrative convention for prophetic resurrection accounts in Kings.
32: The scene at the house — the child lying dead on the prophet’s own bed — sets up the confrontation with death in the private sacred space. Ancient Near Eastern background: the sleeping chambers of gods (in temple ideology) or holy men were associated with life-giving power; placing the dead child there was the Shunammite’s implicit claim on that power. The detail that the bed is specifically Elisha’s (miškāb hāʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm) is not incidental — it establishes the ritual claim.
33: Elisha’s prayer in the closed room mirrors Elijah’s pattern in 1 Kings 17:20–21; prophetic resurrection involves direct address to YHWH in private. The closed door (as also in v4’s oil miracle) is a Sitz im Leben of prophetic concentration — withdrawal from the public sphere to address YHWH. The two-stage pattern of Elisha’s prayer (v33) followed by physical action (v34) reflects the view that resurrection power flows from YHWH in response to prophetic petition rather than from the prophet’s own power.
34: Elisha’s act of lying on the child — mouth to mouth, eyes to eyes, hands to hands (pîw ʿal-pîw wᵉʿênāyw ʿal-ʿênāyw wᵉkappāyw ʿal-kappāyw) — matches exactly Elijah’s action in 1 Kings 17:21, establishing Elisha as Elijah’s true successor who performs the same acts of power. This physical positioning is not a primitive medical procedure (though the specificity is noted in medical history); it is a prophetic act of complete identification with the dead child, transmitting prophetic life-power through contact. The child’s flesh warming indicates the gradual return of life.
35: The two-stage miracle — Elisha rises, walks back and forth, then stretches over the child a second time — follows a pattern of prophetic persistence. The child’s sneezing seven times before opening his eyes is a detail of biological verisimilitude that has no ANE parallel. The number seven has conventional completeness in Hebrew idiom, but sneezing as a sign of returning life appears in some ancient texts (cf. the Egyptian story of Setne and the sneezing revived person). The physical detail anchors the miracle in observed bodily experience.
36: Elisha’s instruction ‘Take up your son’ (śᵉʾî binnēḵ) in the third person through Gehazi restores normal social protocol after the miracle — the Shunammite is now addressed as a mother receiving her child, not as a petitioner receiving a prophetic gift. The mother’s falling at Elisha’s feet (wattipōl ʿal-raglāyw) is gratitude in the same posture as petition (v27) — she who seized his feet in desperate appeal now bows before him in thanksgiving.
37: The Shunammite ‘went out’ with her son — the narrative closure returns her to the domestic space without further explanation. The economy of the ending is deliberate: the account is complete when the gift is restored; theological reflection is not supplied. The reader is expected to draw the connection between YHWH as the giver and restorer of life (v14–17 as promise; 2 Kings 4:32–37 as fulfillment) and the prophetic role as mediator of that life-power.
38: Gilgal (probably the one near Jericho, one of Elisha’s circuit stops; cf. 2 Kings 2:1) during a famine — famines are periodically attested in the Levant through both biblical and extrabiblical sources. The ‘sons of the prophets sitting before him’ (yōšᵉbîm lᵉpānāyw) describes a teaching or consultative session in the prophetic community. Elisha’s instruction to cook a large pot (sir haggādōl) for the group reflects communal meal-sharing within the prophetic guild.
39: The ‘wild vine’ and the wild gourds gathered from it (paqquʿōt śādeh) are most commonly identified with the colocynth (Citrullus colocynthis), a desert plant producing attractive but intensely bitter and violently purgative fruit. In famine conditions, wild foods were gathered indiscriminately; the gatherer’s inexperience or desperation leads him to collect a harmful plant. The gourds sliced into the stew introduce a toxin that would produce acute gastric distress consistent with the men’s reaction.
40: ‘Death in the pot’ (māwet bassîr) — the men recognize the bitter, burning taste characteristic of colocynth poisoning. ANE herbal knowledge included recognition of toxic plants; the immediate identification and the refusal to eat further indicates practical culinary experience. The men’s appeal to Elisha as ‘man of God’ frames the situation as a crisis requiring prophetic intervention — they cannot consume their only available food.
41: Elisha’s use of flour (qemaḥ) to neutralize the toxin has generated discussion about its mode of action. The text does not present this as chemical neutralization but as a prophetic sign-act: the flour is the vehicle of the miracle, not its cause. Similar sign-acts where an unlikely material mediates divine power appear throughout Elisha’s ministry (salt purifying the spring in 2 Kings 2:21; a stick making iron float in 2 Kings 6:6). The point is that YHWH acts through prophetic command even using ordinary materials.
42: Baal-shalishah — a location in Ephraim (cf. 1 Samuel 9:4), distinct from the great cult center Baal-Peor. The name’s connection to the Baalized toponymy of northern Israel is noted but does not affect the account. The offering of twenty barley loaves from firstfruits (leḥem bikkûrîm) is significant: firstfruits were consecrated to YHWH and typically brought to a sanctuary or a recognized representative of divine service. Bringing firstfruits to Elisha confirms his status as the legitimate locus of prophetic-priestly authority in the northern kingdom during this period.
43: The servant’s objection ‘How can I set this before a hundred men?’ establishes the scarcity: twenty loaves for a hundred people is a fifth of normal provision. Elisha’s response — ‘Give it to the people to eat, for thus says the LORD’ — invokes the prophetic formula (kōh ʾāmar yhwh) as the ground for an impossible instruction. The multiplication miracle with its ‘and they had some left over’ (cf. John 6:12–13) follows the same pattern as the widow’s oil: divine provision exceeds the apparent limit.
44: The closing ‘according to the word of the LORD’ (kidbar yhwh) attributes the multiplication explicitly to YHWH’s word spoken through Elisha. The Baal-shalishah feeding functions as a prophetic sign in the tradition of the wilderness manna: YHWH provides for his people through the prophetic word even in conditions of scarcity. The detail of ‘some left over’ echoes the wilderness surplus (Exodus 16:17–18) and anticipates Gospel multiplication accounts where surplus remainder confirms the divine origin of the provision.
